column2: And she walked off toward the apple trees.
Already from the orchard she texted Lilichka to bring her some shoes and something decent to wear to the apple trees.
When the red Honda appeared on the horizon, Miyechka ran out of the apple orchard, where she'd been waiting for rescue, and ran to meet the car, waving her not-yet-dry shorts.
— Pick up the madwoman!
— she started banging on Lilichka's window, showing that she was in nothing but her underwear.
Lilichka laughed, threw a black-and-white dress with a brooch on the chest into her face and said:
— Get dressed!
While Miyechka put on the dress and shook out her wet hair to arrange it into something decent on her head, Lilichka said:
— Right, we have twenty minutes to spare.
I'm not going to tell anyone that I picked you up by the orchard in someone else's shirt and your underwear.
And they sat in the car for another twenty minutes, listening to music loudly.
Lilichka knew that her sister wouldn't tell her anything right now, and Miyechka was grateful to her for that.
The Battle with the Ivy
Lusya was playing outside.
Marta and Maria sat on the old terrace, which was laid with boards down to the grass.

The grass was constantly trying to take over the terrace, and while the grandmother surrendered to it, Marta fought daily with the knotgrass, the dandelions and the field grass, winning back the terrace boards for herself.
The wood had grown dull and to the touch of bare feet was warm and rough.
Marta was constantly being jostled in different directions by her children.
And she always sat frowning.
Her eyebrows, like the grass, were also winning back their place on her face.
She had arrived with evenly plucked eyebrows like wings, and now these wings had grown out all the way to her eyelids, and she looked even sterner than she really was.
Her hair was growing out too.
The even line of locks, black as her brows, was getting lower and lower.
Evening was descending onto the terrace.
Maria soon carried Lusya, with her dirty feet, inside — the lights hadn't been turned on yet in the house, and all the rooms were evenly gray: the dusk was just settling in there.
Lusya went limp, didn't laugh, didn't cry, but with hazy eyes watched Maria as she washed each of her little toes in a bowl of warm water.

After a while they went into the far little room, where since Ustym had fixed the roof there hadn't been a single drop of water, except that in the vase, where a single early sunflower stood, helplessly drooping its head in the dusk.
Maria lay down beside Lusya and began to talk to her about sleep, about night, about the stars and the still water on the river, where the water lilies bloom with small yellow flowers.
Lusya was silent, her eyes losing more and more of their clarity, and she, suddenly letting go of Maria's hand, fell asleep.
This moment, when one second she's still with you, and now she has flown off to her own sleepy lands, always amazed Maria.
How strange it is, when a person falls asleep beside you, to suddenly find yourself alone with yourself.
Now, with no one anymore, Maria looked into the gray window, against which equally gray branches insistently tapped, and fell asleep herself.
Marta just sat there on the terrace — on a stool, leaning her back against the wall.
She had found a few more of the grandfather's shirts — in color they ranged from light blue to blue, some with shiny stripes, on a few silver cufflinks remained.
She no longer fit into her own clothes, so right now she wore the grandfather's pale-blue shirt.
'What a pity,' Marta thought, 'that you can't see where the grandfather went in this shirt — in the black-and-white photographs they all looked equally gray.'
The children calmed down, and she looked straight ahead, waiting to see whether Maria would come out to her again.

Maria didn't come, and Marta looked at her slightly tanned legs, tucked her hair behind her ears and watched how, inevitably, first the cherry trees darkened, then the walnut, then the grass, and finally the sky itself turned dark gray.
She walked around the house and looked into the little room with the sunflower.
It was quiet and still there.
In the room Lusya's face stood out as a pale patch.
Maria, then, had turned to face the wall.

Marta then went down, fending off the ivy that crept up and grabbed her legs, pushing aside the raspberry that scratched her neck and tried to strangle her, forcing her way through the acacias that tore at her hands.
Darkness had settled in the bushes.
She reached the water, a small rise where there was no wild blackberry, raspberry or ivy, because willows towered over the rise and shared their light with no other creature.

From here the house seemed small to her, as if it had shrunk entirely, as if the ivy had wrapped around it completely and the bricks had crumbled, the roof had sagged and in that place there was now nothing.

Marta stood caught off guard by a sudden vision, as if there were not a single living soul around, as if the river had already become completely overgrown with reeds, and in those reeds foxes had already bred, hunting the birds that now and then rose up timidly and vanished into the darkness.
And their house is gone, there's not a single living soul: everyone has scattered, gotten lost, and there's nowhere left to come to when you feel like a loser.
Horror rolled over Marta, she felt as lonely as never before.
She tried to remind herself that she was alive, looked at her own hands, at her shoes, strained to remember where she'd gotten these moccasins, where they had come to her from.
— Martusya, — said the grandfather's voice from under the willows.
— And I wanted to name you Marusya.
— You already have a Marusya, — Marta whispered.
— Martusya, my girl.
Marta did not turn around, looked at her feet and did not see the house, did not see where to go — instead of the house there were vague ruins, some dark mound that was frightening even to approach.
— Martusya, — the voice called, — look, the cottage is gone.
— I see.
The cottage was gone, but, without turning around, Marta walked through the bushes, which again grabbed her legs, strangled her and wouldn't let her pass.
Already in the darkness she felt out the wall.
Outside it was so quiet that Marta could hear her heart pounding.

The wall was wrapped in hops, which scratched her hands, but she somehow made her way along the wall to the very threshold, searched for the peeling paint on the door, but instead of it there was, for some reason, something smooth and white.
She went on and, forcing her way through the lilac thickets, felt out the door of the other entrance.
Tugging at it, she realized that it was locked.
Someone's footsteps were approaching, and at last Marta saw Maria — she stood there frightened:
— Martusya?
Marta, is that you?
Marta came into the house, she was shaking.
— What's wrong with you?
— Nothing, I got cold.
— Are you going to sleep?
— I will.
In the next room, without even washing her feet, Marta covered herself head and all and, listening to the children crawling about inside her, couldn't fall asleep for a long time.
— The pumpkins are practically placed, — Lilichka reported from behind the monitor the next morning.
— Wonderful, — said Marta.
She was holding a cup, her hands were shaking.
— Marta isn't feeling well somehow, — Maria started to say.
— I'm fine, — Marta interrupted her.
— I just slept badly.
All four women glanced at Marta, but none of them said anything.
— I dreamed… of grandfather, — Marta spoke up.
— What did he say?
— asked the grandmother.
— Something about the cottage.

The grandmother sometimes managed to take offense at the grandfather, when he occasionally came to the children and grandchildren in their dreams and didn't mention her with a single word, didn't ask how she was.
— He should have known that I'm already ninety-seven, — when speaking of her own age, she always added a year to herself.
Marta got up from the table and went to the garage.
Everyone followed her with their eyes.
— Marta's, well, — said Lilichka.
— You know what I mean.
Marta would sometimes have her spells.
But this time it wasn't a spell.
That wasn't the matter.
— And why is she all scratched up?
— asked the grandmother.
— Well, yesterday evening she was scrambling through the bushes for some reason, — retorted Maria.
— She was probably looking at the evening river, — said Miyechka.
— You can't get there anymore without the bushes.
Marta went into the garage.
There stood a cabinet of light wood — all stuffed with books that no one would ever read.
Nor had read, to be quite honest.
In the cabinet's mirror Marta saw herself — beautiful, all of yesterday's scratches blurred in the dusty mirror.
The light fell from the heavy garage doors onto one side.
On that side her hair gleamed.
Perfectly straight and heavy.
— Like a Gypsy woman's, — Marta thought, — so black.
Behind the cabinet in the corner she found pruning shears.
Here's one.
Green.
The second blue.
And the third — a little yellow one.
She gathered all the shears against herself and went back to the table.
— Right, let's go.
— Martusya, — the grandmother spoke up.
— Pregnant women mustn't cut anything!
— Why?
— Well, who knows why, some nonsense, probably.
— Got it.
Lilichka and Miyechka got up and followed Marta.
Maria, the grandmother and Lusya remained at the table outside.
— Listen, — said Marta.
— Yesterday under the willows I heard grandfather.
— How's that?
— asked Lilichka.
— Well, how, he was sitting there and saying that the cottage couldn't be seen.
— Marta, are you sure you're okay?
— asked Lilichka.
— Let's clear away these damn ivies, — Marta didn't answer the question, because she was not okay.
— Lilya, — said Miyechka, — just go and dress normally first.
— What, am I dressed abnormally?
— Dress normally, I say!
— Miyechka repeated.
— What's the matter?
— Oh, just put on some rags, because you infuriate everyone with your tidiness, — evidently Miyechka was not in the best mood that morning either.
— I once had a colleague, — Marta began, — who, for every picnic, every mountain hike, or wherever she went, dressed perfectly.
Her sneakers always stayed white.
What a bitch she was, words can't describe it.
— I'll go put on my eighth-grade shorts, — said Lilichka.
— Perfect!
— Solomiya laughed.
And while Lilichka went to look for rags, Mia and Marta set about the ivy.
The ivy resisted.
It put up a fight.
They tore at it with shears and with their hands.
The ivy was of such length and strength that this work seemed a futile struggle against an implacable force of nature.
— I wanted to tell you to focus on your pregnancy.
It's such a time, — Solomiya began.
— Mm-hm.
And Lusya talks to herself all the time.
— I'm worried.
— And what's it to you?
She's mine, after all.
— Marta, she's ours.
— We'll clear the cottage, and then we'll have to think about how to harvest the pumpkins.
— Meaning?
Lilichka came in faded green shorts with a rose on the backside.
On top she wore a T-shirt with a collar studded with pearls.
In it she had played the role of the Snow Maiden at school.
Over the T-shirt she put on a blue sports jacket with white stripes along the sleeves, so the blackberry wouldn't scratch her arms.
— Lord, — Miyechka laughed, — it's perfect!
Lilichka threw back her head and leaned against the wall, raising one shoulder.
Marta glanced at her with a smile and said:
— Get chopping the blackberry.
— What were you saying about the pumpkins?
— Miyechka had already moved around the corner of the house and was tearing the hops off the windows.
— We won't harvest them ourselves.
— Oh, I have such an idea, — Lilichka began.
— Let's put out an ad so all sorts of newfangled hipsters come and carry our super-organic vegan pumpkins for us.
In return we'll feed them, light a bonfire.
Something like that.
A village retreat.
— Lilichka, you were always a bit, you know, — Miyechka spoke up.
— And now you say something like this.
What, live with them?
— It could be for just one night.
— There's no trusting people like that, — retorted Marta.
— We could use a machete here.
That evening the cottage could be seen from under the willows.
It turned out there was ornamental white brick around the windows.
No one had ever seen this.
This time everyone went to the willows together, along the path the sisters had cut through the bushes.
— Tomorrow it'll be exactly the same as it was, — said the grandmother, who always knew how to encourage.
— All this greenery is invincible, girls.

The girls, scratched by ivy, blackberry and wild raspberry, looked at the cottage, which after all peeked out from the greenery, and so Marta managed to hold back the relentless horror that was rising inside her.
Marta walked quickly on ahead along the path, paying no attention to how the freshly cut bushes pricked her calves.
Lilichka and the grandmother walked last.
The grandmother walked slowly, and the path was too narrow to be able to lean on Lilichka's arm.
— Go on ahead, if you want, — said the grandmother.
— I'll walk with you, Granny.
Lilichka lingered, looking at these boundless thickets.
It was evening already.
